stories.headline: Denver Water Faces $30-70 Million Revenue Loss as Stage 1 Drought Restrictions Take Hold
stories.summary:
Denver Water has announced preliminary estimates of $30 million to $70 million in lost revenue as a result of Stage 1 drought restrictions, with the agency planning surcharges designed to penalize high-volume outdoor water use while keeping prices for drinking, cooking and bathing water unchanged. The surcharges, which must be approved by the board this month, could range from as little as $7 per bill for conservation-minded homeowners to as much as $76 per month for those with heavy outdoor water use.

Colorado's snowpack has dropped to record-low levels, with the thin snowpack melting sooner than normal due to a scorching spring. Reservoir storage remains at roughly 80% of average statewide, but officials warn that heavy water use could drain those reserves too quickly and potentially cause major shortages heading into next year if conditions remain dry.

Denver Water, which serves more than 1.5 million people across the city and southern and western suburbs, plans to use a portion of its cash reserves to offset lower water sales and has also taken steps to reduce other costs, such as leaving job vacancies open longer. Cities are hoping the surcharges will help customers reduce water use by 10% to 20%.

Not all metro-area cities are following Denver's approach. Colorado Springs, which has permanent three-day-per-week watering rules, does not plan to impose a drought surcharge at this time. Researchers note that drought fees can promote lasting changes in water use behavior, citing a lingering conservation effect seen in Colorado for several years after the 2002 drought.

stories.headline: Suspect Arrested on Suspicion of Murder in South Thornton Stabbing
stories.summary:
Thornton police have arrested a 25-year-old man on suspicion of murder in connection with a deadly stabbing that occurred in late March. Officers responded to a 911 call about a bleeding person near East 88th Avenue and Grant Street just before 11 p.m. on March 25, and the 29-year-old victim was transported to the hospital, where he later died.

Investigators determined the victim died as a result of a stabbing and identified Zamien Rivas as the suspect. Rivas was arrested Thursday and booked into the Adams County Jail, according to the Thornton Police Department.

The department announced the arrest Friday, nearly 10 days after the initial incident. No further details about the circumstances of the stabbing or the relationship between Rivas and the victim were released.

stories.headline: Truck Driver Charged With Careless Driving After Littleton Gas Station Crash; Brakes Found to Be Functional
stories.summary:
A 33-year-old truck driver faces a charge of careless driving resulting in bodily injury after Littleton police determined that the semitrailer he was operating had functioning brakes at the time of a March 10 crash on Santa Fe Drive. The driver had told police his brakes had failed, but an investigation found no evidence of brake failure.

The semitrailer crossed a median after exiting onto Belleview Avenue, struck a Jeep, and then collided with a QuikTrip gas station at 2338 W. Belleview Ave. The driver of the Jeep was initially in serious condition but has since returned home and is expected to make a full recovery. None of the approximately 30 customers and employees inside the station were injured.

The charge is a misdemeanor. Littleton police did not identify the driver, consistent with the Denver Post's policy of not naming people charged with misdemeanors.

stories.headline: City Park Jazz Will Proceed With 40th Anniversary Season Using Mobile Stage After Bandshell Fire
stories.summary:
City Park Jazz organizers announced the summer concert series will proceed as planned using a mobile stage after a fire on March 26 destroyed the historic 97-year-old bandshell. The fire is suspected to be arson. The mobile stage will be rented for $30,000 to cover all 10 weeks of the series, running June 7 through August 9.

The rental cost will take a significant bite out of City Park Jazz's annual budget of nearly $200,000, but organizers say canceling the season was never considered. Mighty Fine Productions, which has handled sound at City Park Jazz since the 1990s, located the mobile stage. The final location of the stage is still being worked out with Denver Parks and Recreation.

Fundraising efforts have gained momentum in the week since the fire, with more than $40,000 raised across multiple organizations. City Park Jazz's own fundraiser has brought in about $10,000, City Park Friends and Neighbors has raised approximately $10,000, and the Denver Park Trust has surpassed $20,000 toward a $75,000 goal.

The total cost to rebuild the bandshell has not been finalized, but organizers estimate approximately $250,000 and hope the city's insurance will cover most of that amount. Denver Parks and Recreation is reviewing permits for other groups who had scheduled events at the bandshell and working with them to reschedule, relocate or provide refunds.

stories.headline: Colorado Court of Appeals Rules Douglas County Commissioners Violated Open Meetings Law
stories.summary:
The Colorado Court of Appeals ruled Thursday that Douglas County commissioners violated the state's open meetings law by holding 13 private meetings between December 2024 and April 2025 during which they discussed public business, including a home rule charter ballot measure, without providing public notice or allowing public observation. The ruling reversed a lower court decision that had found no violation.

The lawsuit was brought by former Commissioner Lora Thomas, Democratic state Rep. Bob Marshall, who represents Highlands Ranch, and unaffiliated voter Julie Gooden. The plaintiffs had sought to halt a June 2025 special election on the home rule charter question, but that request was denied; voters overwhelmingly defeated the measure on June 24, 2025.

The appellate court found that at least one meeting included a county attorney presentation on the advantages and disadvantages of Douglas County becoming a home rule county, triggering open meetings requirements. The court also ruled that certain closed-door executive sessions were improperly conducted because they included extensive discussion of the home rule charter.

Douglas County indicated the matter may not be resolved, stating the appellate court has ordered additional hearings at the trial court level and that the county is considering whether to return to the lower court or appeal to the Colorado Supreme Court.

stories.headline: Colorado Lost 98 Companies to Relocations Since 2019, Chamber Report Finds
stories.summary:
A new report from the Colorado Chamber of Commerce found that 98 companies have relocated out of the state since 2019, with businesses citing regulatory burden and talent shortages as primary reasons for leaving. The findings underscore ongoing concerns among business leaders about Colorado's competitiveness as a place to operate.

The report adds to a string of recent economic indicators reflecting uncertainty in the state's business climate. An earlier survey by the CU Leeds School of Business found that business leaders' economic outlook had improved only slightly and remained negative overall.

The chamber's findings are expected to fuel debate at the state legislature, where multiple bills affecting business regulation and labor are currently under consideration.

stories.headline: Lakewood Navigation Center Opens After $11 Million Renovation, Offers 24/7 Shelter and Services
stories.summary:
Lakewood officially launched the VOA Colorado Navigation Center on Friday following nearly a year of renovations totaling approximately $11 million at 8000 W. Colfax Ave., a site that previously served as a daytime extreme-weather shelter. The facility, operated in partnership with Volunteers of America Colorado, has been accepting residents since March 10 and can house up to 100 people in its residential building.

The center operates 24 hours a day, seven days a week, and offers showers, lockers, laundry services, and connections to long-term housing resources. The Colorado Department of Local Affairs contributed $9.3 million toward the project. Mayor Wendi Strom called the facility the first of its kind in Jefferson County.

The center is designed to work in tandem with outreach programs such as the Lakewood Police Department's Navigator Program, which identifies people seeking help and connects them to the facility. Drug use is prohibited and a curfew is in place; the center is not a rehabilitation facility and will not shelter families with children.

The opening comes as Jefferson County reported a 27% increase in homelessness between 2024 and 2025, the largest rate increase in the Denver metro area. A 2024 point-in-time count found 329 homeless people in Lakewood and 925 across Jefferson County, compared to 6,539 in Denver.

stories.headline: Colorado National Guard Deploys More Than 200 Soldiers to Middle East for Peacekeeping Mission
stories.summary:
More than 200 soldiers from the Colorado Army National Guard's 1st Battalion, 157th Infantry Regiment are deploying to the Middle East to assist in a longstanding peacekeeping operation between Egypt and Israel. A departure ceremony was held at the Wings Over the Rockies Air and Space Museum at 7711 East Academy Boulevard in Denver.

The deployment is part of an ongoing multinational peacekeeping effort in the region. No specific timeline for the deployment was announced. The 1st Battalion, 157th Infantry Regiment is based in Colorado.

stories.headline: Sheridan School District Cancels Classes Through End of Week as Teachers Strike
stories.summary:
The Sheridan School District canceled classes through the end of the week after teachers began a strike Wednesday. The work stoppage has closed schools in the small Adams County district south of Denver, affecting students and families during what was expected to be a normal school week.

Details about the specific contract disputes driving the strike were not immediately available, but teacher strikes in Colorado have frequently centered on compensation, benefits and working conditions. Sheridan is a small, high-need district serving a predominantly low-income student population.

The cancellations leave families scrambling for alternative childcare and learning arrangements. No resolution to the labor action had been announced as of Friday.

stories.headline: Red Flag Warning Issued for Denver and Eastern Colorado Amid Strong Winds and Low Humidity
stories.summary:
The National Weather Service issued a Red Flag Warning covering Denver and the entire eastern half of Colorado on Friday, citing a dangerous combination of strong winds, low humidity and dry vegetation. Wind gusts were expected to reach 45 mph along the urban corridor, with isolated gusts up to 60 mph possible in the foothills.

The warning remained in effect until 7 p.m. Friday. Meteorologists described the air mass as unusually dry, conditions that could allow a fire to spread rapidly with little warning.

Despite the dangerous fire weather Friday, forecasters said conditions are expected to improve over the Easter weekend, with lighter winds and warmer temperatures expected through the holiday.

stories.headline: Ancient Bristlecone Pines on Mount Blue Sky Estimated to Be Up to 1,600 Years Old
stories.summary:
As the Mount Blue Sky Scenic Byway prepares to reopen in the coming weeks, attention is turning to the Mount Goliath Natural Area, located about three miles from the fee station at the base of the 14,268-foot peak. The area is home to Rocky Mountain bristlecone pine trees estimated by the USDA to be between 700 and 1,600 years old, making them among the oldest living organisms on the planet.

The trees' distinctive twisted and gnarled appearance results from centuries of growth in wind-blasted, drought-prone conditions near treeline. The roughly 160-acre natural area features the Bristlecone Loop Trail, a quarter-mile path that allows visitors to view the ancient trees up close.

The reopening of the byway is expected to draw significant traffic to the area, offering Colorado residents and tourists the chance to experience both the scenic drive and the remarkable natural history of Mount Goliath.

stories.headline: Colorado Springs Mayor Mobolade Used Security Detail and City Vehicle for Personal Errands, Audit Finds
stories.summary:
A report by the Colorado Springs City Auditor found five instances in 2025 in which Mayor Yemi Mobolade's security detail accompanied him on personal activities, including trips to pick up dry cleaning and transport his children from school, before or between official city events. The report, approved by the city's Audit Committee on March 26, also found that the mayor's wife used a city vehicle for personal travel in May and June 2025.

The Audit Committee noted that elected officials like Mobolade are not classified as city employees, meaning policies around personal use of city resources were not clearly defined at the time. The report found the incidents had mitigating factors but recommended the city establish clear, enforceable policy governing elected officials' use of city resources, including police details and vehicles.

In a statement Friday, Mobolade said the report confirmed city resources had been used appropriately and that his administration would consider the Audit Committee's recommendations. City Council members were divided, with at least one calling the findings an abuse of power and misappropriation of funds.

Mobolade was elected mayor in 2023 and is scheduled to kick off his reelection campaign on April 6. The investigation originated from an anonymous tip submitted to the City Auditor's fraud and waste hotline.

joke.joke: According to most studies, people's number one fear is public speaking. Number two is death. This means to the average person, if you go to a funeral, you're better off in the casket than doing the eulogy.
